senses.definition: something that is a typical or normal part of a person's job

examples.x: After years of working in the ER, the doctor had seen it all, and to her, saving a life was just all in a day's work.
Dealing with difficult customers is all in a day's work for a customer service representative.
Responding to urgent emails late at night is all in a day's work for an executive.
The firefighter rescued the cat from the tree, but to him, it was all in a day's work.

senses.definition: used when one is back to doing one's job and normal activities at work, particularly after a leave or vacation

examples.x: By the time the new CEO arrived, the employees had already been in harness and had improved the company's performance.
The team is in harness and working together to complete the project by the deadline.
They were in harness and successfully closed the biggest deal of the year.

senses.definition: to combine enjoyable activities with one's work

examples.x: He regrets mixing business with pleasure by dating his co-worker, which eventually led to a messy breakup.
She's always careful not to mix business with pleasure when she's out networking.
They've been successful at mixing business with pleasure by hosting client dinners at their favorite restaurant.

senses.definition: a draining and stressful lifestyle that consists of constantly competing with others for success, wealth, power, etc. and so leaving no room for rest and pleasure

examples.x: By the time he retired, he had been in the rat race for over three decades and was ready for a slower pace of life.
He burned out after years of participating in the rat race and decided to take a sabbatical.
Many people feel trapped in the rat race of their jobs and long for more meaningful work.
She has been stuck in the rat race for years, working long hours and sacrificing her personal life for her career.

senses.definition: to have work-related discussions outside of work, particularly when it is annoying or inappropriate

examples.x: By the time they finished talking shop, it was already late and they had missed their train.
During the conference, they spent most of their time talking shop and exchanging business cards.
They have been talking shop for hours, but haven't made much progress on their project.
Whenever they get together, they can't resist talking shop and discussing the latest industry trends.
She gets bored when her friends talk shop about their tech jobs.

word_list.word: the right (hand|) does not know what the left (hand|) [is] doing
word_list.pronunciation: ðə ɹˈaɪt hˈænd ɔːɹ dʌznˌɑːt nˈoʊ wˌʌt ðə lˈɛft hˈænd ɔːɹ ɪz dˈuːɪŋ

senses.definition: used to refer to the lack of communication between the different parts of an organization about their roles or activities that leads to confusion and dysfunction
official procedures or rules that are unnecessary and time-consuming

examples.x: The company has been struggling to get the necessary permits due to all the red tape involved in the process.
The project was delayed for months due to all the red tape involved in getting approval from the government.
They have been dealing with red tape for weeks, trying to get their visas approved for their trip.
The new regulations added even more red tape to an already complex system.
They had to navigate through a lot of red tape to get their visa approved.

senses.definition: to no longer make new entries when an accounting period is at its end

examples.x: By the time they realized they needed to close the books, it was already too late.
The accounting team worked long hours to close the books for the year and meet the deadline.

senses.definition: conflict between the people of an organization or company that leads to unpleasant situations

examples.x: By the time they realized there was blood on the carpet, it was already too late to prevent the damage.
If they don't address the problem soon, there will be blood on the carpet and it will be much harder to resolve.
The CEO called an emergency meeting because there's blood on the carpet and they need to address the problem immediately.
There was blood on the carpet after the heated discussion, and it took a while for things to calm down.
They have been trying to avoid blood on the carpet, but the problem has been building up for months.

senses.definition: a person who holds the highest rank in a particular group or organization

examples.x: By the time they realized they weren't the top dog anymore, it was too late to catch up.
He's the top dog in the company and has a lot of influence over its direction.
They have been trying to become the top dog in their field for years, and are finally starting to make progress.
They were the top dog in the industry for years, but lost their position after a series of scandals.

senses.definition: a way of referring to the woman who is a leading member or the founder of an organization, movement, etc.
a person or thing that has recently joined a specific place, field, company, group, etc.

examples.x: By the time they realized they weren't the new kid on the block anymore, they had already established themselves as a major player in the industry.
The new kid on the block is already making waves with their innovative approach to marketing.
They have been the new kid on the block for a few months now, but they're starting to get the hang of things.
When they were the new kid on the block, they struggled to establish themselves in the competitive market.

senses.definition: something or someone that has been at a place for so long that one no longer notices them

examples.x: By the time they left the company, they had become part of the furniture and were deeply missed.
He had been the head of the department for so many years that everyone considered him part of the furniture.
If they stay in the same job for too long, they risk becoming part of the furniture and losing their edge.
She's been working here for so long that she's practically part of the furniture.
They have been living in the neighborhood for so long that they're practically part of the furniture.

senses.definition: to control and manage an organization, group, business, etc. in a manner that is very strict, efficient, and effective

examples.x: By the time they left the company, they had been running a tight ship for years and had earned a reputation for being disciplined and efficient.
She runs a tight ship at the office and expects everyone to meet their deadlines.
They have been running a tight ship at the factory and have been able to increase productivity.
When he was in charge, he ran a tight ship and was able to turn the company around.

word_list.pronunciation: fˈɔːl ˈaʊt wɪð wˈʌnz bɹˈɛd ænd bˈʌɾɚ

senses.definition: to have a disagreement with someone or something that provides one's primary source of income or livelihood

examples.x: He fell out with his bread and butter when he refused to work on the weekends.
If you're not careful, you could fall out with your bread and butter and be out of a job.
She's afraid to speak up because she doesn't want to fall out with her bread and butter.
They've been fighting a lot lately, and she's worried she'll fall out with her bread and butter.

senses.definition: a period of time in which someone no longer works due to old age

examples.x: Many people save money throughout their careers for their golden years.
The couple spent their golden years traveling the world together.
She looks forward to spending her golden years with her grandchildren.
Retirement homes are designed to make the golden years more comfortable.
He moved to a quiet countryside house to enjoy his golden years.

senses.definition: a job that does not provide one with the chance to advance to a better position or job

examples.x: After years of working in dead-end jobs, she decided to go back to school and pursue a new career.
He felt stuck in a dead-end job with no prospect of promotion or advancement.
He was tired of working long hours in a dead-end job with no chance of a pay raise or promotion.
Many young people today are struggling to find work that is not just a dead-end job with no future prospects.
She realized that the dead-end job she had been working in for years was not fulfilling her desire for a meaningful and challenging career.

senses.definition: a period of leisure or vacation spent engaging in activities related to one's job or profession
people who are newly employed or admitted in order to provide the group, company, etc. with enthusiasm and new and exciting ideas

examples.x: The company was in need of new blood, so they hired a team of young, innovative employees to bring new ideas and energy to the workplace.
The department was struggling with outdated practices and procedures, so they hired a consultant to bring in new blood and help them modernize their approach.
The organization was stagnating, and it was clear that they needed new blood to bring fresh perspectives and ideas to the table.
The political party needed to attract new blood to remain relevant, so they launched a campaign to appeal to younger voters.
The sports team brought in new blood by signing several promising young players, hoping to inject new energy into the team.
